markdown:
## Matrix Transformations in Two Dimensions


**Matrix transformations** are a powerful tool in linear algebra that allow us to represent and perform geometric transformations on points in a coordinate system.

### Basic Transformation Matrix


The general form of a **2D matrix transformation** is:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} e \\ f \end{bmatrix} $$

<callout type="hint">Here, $\begin{bmatrix}x \\ y\end{bmatrix}$ represents the **original **point, and the resulting coordinates after transformation are obtained by **multiplying **this vector with the $2\times2$ matrix and **adding **the translation vector $\begin{bmatrix}e \\ f\end{bmatrix}$.</callout>

<callout type="note">The $2\times2$ matrix represents linear transformations such as rotation, reflection, and scaling, while the added vector represents translation.</callout>

### Types of Transformations


#### 1. Reflections


**Reflections **can be represented using matrices. For example, reflection about the **y-axis**:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

<callout type="example">The point $(3,2)$ reflected about the y-axis becomes $(-3,2)$:

\[\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} \]</callout>

#### 2. Stretches


**Horizontal **and **vertical stretches** can be represented by scaling the corresponding coordinate:

* Horizontal stretch by factor k: $\begin{bmatrix}k & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$
* Vertical stretch by factor k: $\begin{bmatrix}1 & 0 \\ 0 & k\end{bmatrix}$

![A square on a coordinate grid stretched horizontally into a rectangle by a matrix transformation.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/0ba0328a97c02ca8858d33413a218772f8e4bb58-842x405.png)

#### 3. Enlargements


An **enlargement **is a uniform scaling in all directions:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} k & 0 \\ 0 & k \end{bmatrix} $$

where $k$ is the scale factor.

![A square on a coordinate grid enlarged by a scale factor of 2.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/d0b0fdcdb3df723db1112a566ba7be2b3f9cf88c-854x393.png)

#### 4. Translations


Translations are represented by the addition of a vector:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} a \\ b \end{bmatrix} $$

<callout type="hint">This translates a point $(x, y)$ by $a$ units horizontally and $b$ units vertically.</callout>

#### 5. Rotations


A **rotation **by angle $\theta$ counterclockwise about the **origin **is represented by:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} $$

<callout type="example">To rotate the point $(1,0)$ by $90^\circ$ counterclockwise:

\[\begin{bmatrix} \cos 90^\circ& -\sin 90^\circ\\\sin 90^\circ&\cos 90^\circ\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \]</callout>

![A square rotated about the origin on a coordinate plane, illustrating a matrix rotation transformation.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/31066dcddbac7af6964a343e53d528de0d3a9417-835x411.png)

### Composition of Transformations


**Multiple transformations** can be combined by multiplying their matrices in the order of application. This is a powerful feature of matrix transformations.

<callout type="example">To reflect about the y-axis and then rotate by $90^\circ$:

\[\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \]

This resulting matrix represents a reflection about the line $y = x$.</callout>

<callout type="note">The order of matrix multiplication matters, as matrix multiplication is not commutative.</callout>

### Iterative Techniques and Fractals


Fractals can be generated using **iterative **matrix transformations. This involves repeatedly applying a set of transformations to a set of points.

<callout type="example">The Sierpinski triangle can be generated using three transformations:

\[ T_1 : \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} \]\[ T_2 : \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \]\[ T_3 : \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \]

Starting with any point and repeatedly applying these transformations (chosen randomly) results in the Sierpinski triangle fractal.</callout>

### Determinant and Area


The **determinant **of a transformation matrix has a **geometric interpretation **related to **area **scaling.

For a $2\times2$ matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix}a & b \\ c & d\end{bmatrix}$, the determinant is:

$$\det A = ad - bc$$

The **absolute value** of the determinant, $|\det A|$, represents the factor by which the transformation scales areas.

$$\text{Area of image} = |\det A| \times \text{Area of original}$$

<callout type="example">For a transformation matrix:

\[ A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \]

\[\det A = 2 \times 3 - 0 \times 0 = 6 \]

This transformation scales areas by a factor of 6.</callout>

<callout type="warning">Students often forget that it's the absolute value of the determinant that matters for area scaling. A negative determinant indicates a change in orientation, for example a reflection.g.However, its magnitude still determines the area scaling.</callout>

<callout type="tip">When studying matrix transformations, always try to visualize the geometric effect of the transformation. This can greatly aid in understanding and problem-solving.</callout>
